summary: The dashboard highlights significant high-risk regulatory updates for food seasoning/snack compliance. Key additions include: Stricter contaminant limits from Codex, Singapore (prohibiting non-compliant levels and specific veterinary drugs), and the US (action levels for aflatoxins, verification for poisonous substances). Additive regulations are tightened in Singapore (SFA safety assessment, prohibition of non-permitted additives) and the US (prohibiting delisted/prohibited additives, strict listing for permitted/GRAS substances). New microbiology verification requirements are in place for the EU (foodborne bacteria, toxins, process hygiene) and GCC (microbiological criteria). Extensive new high-risk labelling and allergen rules are introduced across GCC, India, Middle East (mandatory expiration/shelf-life), Singapore (ingredient/additive declaration), Taiwan (11 specific allergens), Thailand (prohibited foods), and the US (9 major allergens, 'gluten-free' claims, allergen preventive controls, specific declarations for cochineal/carmine, sulfites, and FD&C Yellow No. 5). Moderate-risk additions include new verification requirements for additives, contaminants, microbiology, and general/voluntary labelling across several regions. Several moderate-risk rules were removed, often due to source extraction issues, requiring manual verification.

changes.newLimit: Maximum levels (MLs) must be legally permitted concentrations
changes.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants, which are the maximum concentrations legally permitted in a commodity to protect human health. These levels are kept as low as possible based on scientific evidence.

changes.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
changes.notes: These criteria define the acceptability of a product or a batch of food placed on the market.
These criteria indicate the correct functioning of the production process.

changes.parameter: General labelling requirements, including allergen declaration and warnings
changes.notes: Specific labelling requirements for prepackaged food stuffs, including mandatory information, allergen declarations, and warnings, must be verified by consulting the full text of GSO 9:2022.
Specific mandatory expiration date requirements for food products must be verified by consulting the full text of GSO 150-1:2013.
Specific microbiological limits for various foodstuffs and food ingredients must be verified by consulting the full text of GSO 1016:2015.

changes.newLimit: Verification required as per FSSAI Labelling and Display Regulations

changes.parameter: Mandatory shelf life periods for food products
changes.notes: Mandatory shelf life periods for food products must be verified by consulting the full regulation GSO 150-1:2013.

changes.newLimit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA and permitted for use
changes.notes: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply.
An application can be submitted to SFA to evaluate the use of an additive not currently permitted.

changes.newLimit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
changes.notes: All food businesses must comply with these limits.
Applies if laboratory test results indicate contaminants exceed specified limits.
Prohibition under Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels
changes.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish, sulphite
changes.notes: Food allergen labeling is required if these allergens are present as an ingredient or food additive. For sulphite, the limit applies as residue sulphur dioxide.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited from production, import, or sale. Verification required for specific list of prohibited foods.
changes.notes: Certain foods are legally prohibited from being on the market.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited from use in food, as listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30)
changes.notes: These substances are labeled as 'DELISTED'.

changes.newLimit: Must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173, 73, 74) or evaluated by FEMA/JECFA for flavoring substances
changes.notes: The Substances Added to Food inventory provides a partial list; full compliance requires checking the referenced CFR parts.

changes.newLimit: Must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184)
changes.notes: The Substances Added to Food inventory provides a partial list; full compliance requires checking the referenced CFR parts.

changes.newLimit: Must be approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, as per 21 CFR Part 181
changes.notes: These substances are approved for specific uses under 21 CFR Part 181.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited from use in food, as listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189)
changes.notes: These substances are labeled as 'PROHIBITED' or 'PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS'.
The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
This is an action level. Specific action levels also exist for Brazil nuts, peanuts and peanut products, and pistachio nuts.

changes.parameter: Poisonous or deleterious substances (e.g., toxic elements, mycotoxins, persistent pesticides, radionuclides, other chemical contaminants)
changes.newLimit: Verification required against the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool and referenced guidance documents/regulations (21 CFR parts 109 and 509)
changes.notes: The tool provides a consolidated list of tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Users must refer to linked guidance documents for detailed application.

changes.newLimit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content
changes.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling and established compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the claim.

changes.parameter: Allergen preventive controls and accurate labeling
changes.notes: Facilities must put written procedures in place to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per the CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117).

changes.parameter: Declaration of cochineal extract and carmine
changes.notes: Required as they are identified as allergenic substances.

The food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name OR immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a 'contains' statement. Specific types of tree nuts, fish species, and Crustacean shellfish species must be declared.

changes.parameter: Declaration of sulfiting agents (e.g., sodium bisulfite)
changes.notes: Required due to adverse reactions (e.g., asthma) in sensitive individuals.
Required due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.

A specific Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) provides guidance for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices.

Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable 'cross-contact' only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes and taken every precaution. They must be truthful and not misleading.
